name: Egyptian Arabic (Cairene)

ancestors.notes: Coptic substrate (lexical; debated prosodic effects on the Cairene stress system)

notes: Egyptian Arabic — Cairene (مصري, the prestige urban koine and most widely understood Arabic dialect via film/TV). DEFINING FEATURE: ج jim → /ɡ/ (voiced velar stop, NOT /dʒ/ or /ʒ/) — the single most recognisable Cairene marker; جميل → [ɡaˈmiːl]. ق qaf → /ʔ/ glottal stop in inherited vocabulary (قلب → [ʔalb]), retained as /q/ in learned/Classical borrowings and as /ɡ/ in some Bedouin/loan strata. Interdentals merged: ث θ → /t/ (~/s/ in learned loans), ذ ð → /d/ (~/z/), ظ/ض → /dˤ/ (~/zˤ/ in loans) — four-emphatic inventory /tˤ dˤ sˤ zˤ/. چ (loan grapheme) → /ʒ/ for French/English /ʒ,dʒ/ loans (since native ج is /ɡ/). STRESS: Cairene stress is famously regular and weight-sensitive (final superheavy CVːC/CVCC → final; else rightmost non-final heavy syllable, capped at the antepenult) — this is a syllable-weight algorithm NOT expressible in the ending-based StressRules schema and is left as engine-level future work; it is intentionally NOT encoded here. Source: Watson (2002) The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic; Mitchell (1956) An Introduction to Egyptian Colloquial Arabic; Egyptian Arabic phonology (Wikipedia). EMPHATIC SPREADING now modeled via allophone_rules (AR_EG_EMPH_*): /a aː/ back to [ɑ ɑː] adjacent to an emphatic /tˤ dˤ sˤ zˤ/ — Cairene has famously long-domain emphasis spreading (Watson 2002).

sources.author: Watson, J.C.E.
sources.title: The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic
sources.notes: Cairene as a primary descriptive case; jim→ɡ, qaf→ʔ, regular weight-sensitive stress.

sources.author: Mitchell, T.F.
sources.title: An Introduction to Egyptian Colloquial Arabic
sources.notes: Classic descriptive reference for Cairene phonology and the stress system.

sources.author: Badawi, E.S. & Hinds, M.
sources.title: A Dictionary of Egyptian Arabic
sources.notes: Lexicon with transcriptions confirming jim→ɡ and qaf→ʔ reflexes.

sources.author: Holes, C.
sources.title: Modern Arabic: Structures, Functions, and Varieties

allophone_rules.notes: Emphasis (pharyngealization) spreading: short /a/ backs to [ɑ] when it follows an emphatic consonant /tˤ dˤ sˤ ðˤ (zˤ)/. Watson 2002 (Cairene as primary case) ch. Emphasis; Egyptian emphatics /tˤ dˤ sˤ zˤ/ with strong bidirectional spreading.

Emphasis spreading (leftward): short /a/ backs to [ɑ] before an adjacent emphatic consonant. Watson 2002 (Cairene as primary case) ch. Emphasis; Egyptian emphatics /tˤ dˤ sˤ zˤ/ with strong bidirectional spreading.
Emphasis spreading: long /aː/ backs to [ɑː] after an adjacent emphatic. Watson 2002 (Cairene as primary case) ch. Emphasis; Egyptian emphatics /tˤ dˤ sˤ zˤ/ with strong bidirectional spreading.

Emphasis spreading (leftward): long /aː/ backs to [ɑː] before an adjacent emphatic. Watson 2002 (Cairene as primary case) ch. Emphasis; Egyptian emphatics /tˤ dˤ sˤ zˤ/ with strong bidirectional spreading.
